title: Lesson 5 - Power Features: References, Scripts, and Assets
description: Go beyond plain instructions by bundling templates, automation scripts, and static data with your skill. Learn when to externalize, how to reference files safely, and how to keep everything maintainable.
learningObjectives: Design a reference library that keeps SKILL.md focused while providing battle-tested templates and examples.
Author lightweight scripts the AI can invoke for validation or automation, with clear safeguards.
Package assets (JSON, CSV, configs) and reference them consistently from the instruction body.
Document every supporting file so maintainers understand why it exists.

videos.title: When to Externalize: SKILL.md Isn't Enough
videos.description: Recognize the thresholds for moving templates, checklists, and data out of SKILL.md and into supporting directories.
scriptOutline: Symptoms: SKILL.md exceeding 400 lines, repeated template sections, difficult git diffs.
Apply the decision tree from Lesson 2: references for templates, scripts for automation, assets for data.
Show the before/after: a bloated SKILL.md vs. a clean one with supporting files.
Discoverability: add a 'Supporting Files' table in SKILL.md so the AI and humans know what exists.
Running example: deploy-checklist is getting complex — time to add a reference template.
talkingPoints: Externalizing content reduces noise without losing quality.
Supporting files become canonical references you can share across multiple skills.
Always tell the AI exactly WHAT to read and WHY — don't make it guess.

videos.title: References in Action: Templates and Examples
videos.description: Build a reference library for the deploy-checklist skill with a template and a good/bad example pair.
scriptOutline: Create references/report-template.md for the deploy-checklist skill — the exact format the AI should produce.
Create references/example-good.md showing a correctly formatted deploy report.
Show how to reference them from SKILL.md: 'Read references/report-template.md and follow its structure exactly.'
Naming conventions: use descriptive names (report-template.md, not template1.md).
Demo: test the skill with and without the reference template — show the consistency improvement.
Keep reference files focused: one template per file, not everything in one mega-file.
talkingPoints: references/ is the most commonly used supporting directory — it's where your templates and examples live.
Good/bad example pairs are powerful: the AI learns from contrast, not just positive examples.
One reference file per artifact keeps diffs clean and makes each file easy to update independently.

videos.title: Scripts and Assets: Automation and Data
videos.description: Add a validation script and a configuration asset to the deploy-checklist skill.
scriptOutline: Create scripts/pre-deploy-check.sh: a simple script that checks for uncommitted changes and pending migrations.
Script best practices: descriptive header comment, validate inputs, log progress, exit with meaningful codes.
Create assets/required-env-vars.json: a list of environment variables the deploy-checklist must verify.
Reference both from SKILL.md: 'Run scripts/pre-deploy-check.sh' and 'Read assets/required-env-vars.json'.
Security reminder: scripts run on your machine. Audit third-party scripts before installing.
talkingPoints: Scripts make skills actionable — the AI doesn't just advise, it can validate and verify.
Assets hold ground truth the AI should read but never modify — treat them like configuration.
For non-tech learners: start with a simple script (even one line) and build from there.

textSections.title: Running Example: deploy-checklist with Supporting Files
textSections.content:
Here's our deploy-checklist skill after adding all three directories:

```
deploy-checklist/
├── SKILL.md                          # Instructions (now references supporting files)
├── references/
│   ├── report-template.md            # The exact format for the deploy report
│   └── example-good.md               # A well-formatted example report
├── scripts/
│   └── pre-deploy-check.sh           # Validates git state and migrations
└── assets/
    └── required-env-vars.json        # Env vars to verify per environment
```

**Updated SKILL.md workflow (excerpt):**
```markdown
## Workflow
1. Run `scripts/pre-deploy-check.sh` — verify clean git state and no pending migrations.
2. Read `assets/required-env-vars.json` — verify all listed variables are set for the target environment.
3. Run the test suite and capture pass/fail counts.
4. Generate the deploy report following `references/report-template.md` exactly.
5. See `references/example-good.md` for tone and detail level.
```

The skill went from 'tell me what to check' to 'check it for me and report the results.'

textSections.title: Reference Library Patterns
textSections.content:
Organize references/ for clarity:

| File Pattern | Purpose | Example |
|-------------|---------|--------|
| `template-*.md` | Output structure the AI must follow | `template-report.md` |
| `example-good.md` | What great output looks like | A completed deploy report |
| `example-bad.md` | What to avoid (anti-patterns) | A sloppy, incomplete report |
| `guide-*.md` | Decision guides, style references | `guide-severity-levels.md` |

Keep each file focused on ONE thing. When references/ grows beyond 5-6 files, consider whether the skill is trying to do too much.

textSections.title: Script Guardrails
textSections.content:
Keep scripts safe and predictable:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
# pre-deploy-check.sh
# Purpose: Verify clean git state and no pending migrations before deploy
# Inputs: None
# Outputs: Status report to stdout, exit code 0 (pass) or 1 (fail)

set -euo pipefail  # Exit on error, undefined vars, pipe failures

echo "Checking for uncommitted changes..."
if ! git diff --quiet; then
  echo "FAIL: Uncommitted changes found"
  exit 1
fi

echo "Checking for pending migrations..."
# Add your migration check here

echo "PASS: All pre-deploy checks passed"
exit 0
```

**Rules:**
- Start with a comment: purpose, inputs, outputs.
- Use `set -euo pipefail` for safety.
- Echo progress so the AI can report what happened.
- Exit 0 for success, non-zero for failure.
- Never pipe user arguments into destructive commands without validation.

textSections.title: Referencing Files from SKILL.md
textSections.content:
Always use **relative paths** from the skill directory:

```markdown
# Good — relative paths
Read `references/report-template.md` for the output format.
Run `scripts/pre-deploy-check.sh` to validate pre-conditions.
Load environment requirements from `assets/required-env-vars.json`.

# Bad — absolute paths (break when shared)
Read `/Users/jane/templates/report.md`

# Bad — external URLs (create dependency on network)
Fetch the template from https://example.com/template.md
```

**Why relative paths matter:**
- They work on any machine (your teammate's paths are different from yours)
- They keep the skill self-contained — everything it needs is in the folder
- They work with progressive disclosure — the AI knows exactly where to look

textSections.title: Supporting Files Documentation Table
textSections.content:
Add this to the bottom of your SKILL.md so humans and AI can discover all files:

```markdown
## Supporting Files

| File | Purpose | When to Read |
|------|---------|-------------|
| references/report-template.md | Output format template | Step 4 of workflow |
| references/example-good.md | Tone and detail reference | Step 5 of workflow |
| scripts/pre-deploy-check.sh | Git + migration validation | Step 1 of workflow |
| assets/required-env-vars.json | Required env vars per environment | Step 2 of workflow |
```

This table doubles as a maintenance checklist — if a file is missing from this table, it's either undocumented or unnecessary.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding directories before you need them
commonMistakes.explanation: An empty scripts/ folder adds complexity without value. Wait until you have an actual script to write. Most skills start with just SKILL.md.

commonMistakes.mistake: Using absolute paths to reference files
commonMistakes.explanation: 'references/template.md' works everywhere. '/Users/jane/skills/template.md' breaks the moment someone else uses the skill.

commonMistakes.mistake: Putting everything in one massive reference file
commonMistakes.explanation: One template, one style guide, and three examples in a single file is hard to maintain. Keep each file focused on one thing.
Scripts need execute permissions: 'chmod +x scripts/check.sh'. Without this, the AI can't run them and you'll get a confusing error.
Without a Supporting Files table, maintainers have to guess what each file does. A 5-line table prevents hours of confusion.

commonMistakes.mistake: Scripts that modify files outside the skill directory
commonMistakes.explanation: Skills should validate and report, not silently change things. If a script needs to modify files, make that explicit in SKILL.md so it's reviewable.

reflectionQuestions: Does your skill need supporting files right now, or are you adding them prematurely?
What repetitive validation in your workflow could be automated with a simple script?
If you shared your skill with someone who's never seen it, which reference files would help them understand the expected output?
How would adding a good/bad example pair change the consistency of your skill's output?

exercises.title: Build a Reference Template
exercises.description: Create references/ with a template file that defines your skill's output format. Update SKILL.md to reference it explicitly.
exercises.output: A references/ directory with a template file, and SKILL.md updated to use it.
successCriteria: Reference file contains a clear, complete output template.
SKILL.md workflow includes a step: 'Read references/[file] and follow its structure.'
Tested the skill — confirmed the AI follows the template consistently.
Added the file to the Supporting Files table in SKILL.md.

exercises.title: Add a Validation Script
exercises.description: Create scripts/ with a simple validation script. Wire it into your SKILL.md workflow. Test it manually first, then via the skill.
exercises.output: A scripts/ directory with an executable script, referenced from SKILL.md.
successCriteria: Script has a descriptive header comment (purpose, inputs, outputs).
Script is executable (chmod +x) and tested manually.
SKILL.md workflow includes a step to run the script.
Tested the full skill workflow including the script.

exercises.title: Package an Asset
exercises.description: Add one static data file to assets/ that the AI needs to reference during the workflow (JSON config, checklist data, etc.).
exercises.output: An assets/ directory with a data file, referenced from SKILL.md.
successCriteria: Asset file contains structured data the AI reads during the workflow.
SKILL.md includes a step: 'Read assets/[file] to determine...'
Test confirmed the AI used the asset data correctly.
File documented in the Supporting Files table.

instructorNotes: This lesson is where skills go from 'useful' to 'powerful'. The reference template exercise shows immediate output consistency improvement.
Demo the difference: skill without references (inconsistent output) vs. with template (identical structure every time). The visual contrast is compelling.
For non-tech learners: focus on references/ (everyone can create a template). Position scripts/ as optional for those comfortable with the command line.
Provide a ready-made script boilerplate for learners who've never written a shell script. They can adapt it rather than starting from scratch.
The security discussion around scripts/ should inform, not scare. Frame it as: 'Same caution as downloading any software — know what you're running.'
The Supporting Files table is a sleeper hit — learners who add it report much easier maintenance weeks later.
